speaker: interviewer
text: Hey, good morning. Today is December 12, 1996. Survivor is Sidney Taussig. Interviewer, Mark Goldberg. We're in Huntington, New York in the USA and the language is English. I am the interviewer, Mark Goldberg. Survivor is Sidney Taussig. Today is December 12, 1996. We're in Huntington, New York, USA. Language, English. Again, good morning. And again, this is tape number one. Please state your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sidney Taussig.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell Taussig?

speaker: interviewee
text: T-A-U-S-S-I-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name when you were a little boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Zeenek, Z-E-E-N-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last name was Taussig, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 67 years old now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: October 17, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Prague, Czechoslovakia

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you very much. Tell-- [INAUDIBLE] please tell me what you can remember about your boyhood in Prague, your early memories of home, of your parents, Jewish holidays, school, your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- I lived with my grandparents originally, and my grandfather was a very orthodox man. He was a, a butcher, a kosher butcher. And-- --my father also was a butcher by trade. At the time, people with trades were professionals. So he had to take four years of apprenticeship to become a butcher. When he married my, my mother, he still stayed with the-- helped my grandfather. But later he started to do contracting. He bought himself a couple horses and then another pair of horses. He had four pair of horses, and then he started with trucks. And he developed full-- became a full contractor. And he bought out the house from my-- from my grandfather. And we stayed in the house, and my grandparents lived across the yard in a-- in a small apartment. In 1935, I, I was just about to start school. My grandfather died. And-- --because my grandmother was alone, I used to sleep over with her so-- because she was afraid. So I used to sleep with her. Then I attended-- I attended grammar school, which is five year in, in Prague. This, the area where we lived was outside-- on the outskirts of Prague. It still was Prague but it wasn't center off Prague. And there were-- it was a mostly Christian area. There were-- there weren't any, any Jewish people. So I was brought up in a non-Jewish environment really. And I went to a public school. My-- most of my friends were Christians. As a matter of fact, before I went to the public school, I attended a, a, a kindergarten school, which was in an abbey. And every time the kids had to pray, the sisters allowed me to go out because I wasn't kneeling. So I, I, I went out. Then, after I finished grammar school, I wanted to go into gymnasium, which is a high school in Europe. It's gymnasium, which is seven or eight years depending if it's a reform, a RIAA school, which is a technical school. Or-- --you want to go to a-- become a doctor, you go through a gymnasium, which is eight years. Well, I wanted to be an engineer and so I chose the RIAA school, which was, again, in Prague. And I took the exams, and I was accepted. But at that time, this was in-- --1941. Hitler came in. And, and Jews were not allowed to go to school. However, my father-- at that time, I was just about to start the gymnasium-- told me, you know, this, this is-- there must be some mistake. You go to school. You start school.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went into the school. I went in-- into the class. And the teacher called out the roll call, all the names. And at the end, there were about six Jewish guys. And when my name came up, naturally, none of the Jewish fellows answered.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when my name came out, I answered. I was the only Jewish guy there. And the teacher said, well, you are excused because we cannot accept any Jewish children. So I packed up and went home. And because there were a lot of Jewish teachers who were also discharged, I got acquainted-- my parents got acquainted with some parents and we formed little circles. Like, we called-- we used to call them circles. First, I wanted to-- there was only one Jewish, Jewish school in central Prague but it was all filled up. There were too many kids and I had no-- I didn't-- my parents didn't know anybody to get some pull to get into the class.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we formed a circle. We went from one house to another. One day we were in this house. Next day we-- and we got a, a, very good education from that.

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as my activities, there were some Zionist organizations in Prague. I never belonged to any Zionist organization. But as it says, misery keeps a company, so I tried to seek out a lot of Jewish kids. And this-- the organization was on the other side of Prague, the name was Strasnice. And it used to belong to an organization, which was called Hagibor. And it was under the leadership of a Jewish fellow. His name was Freddy Hirsch. And I used to go over there, and we used to play soccer there. What happened is, I got a pass because the Jews at that-- we were wearing stars and I got a pass on the trolley car. So from my place, I always used to go to the other part of the-- but I used to, drive-- we used to be able to sit only in the back of the trolley car. And any time a, a German guy or SS man would come and say, all the Jews out. So all the Jews had to, to get out of the trolley car. And that was-- that's how I really lived through the time in Prague with the German. Now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back a little bit. What was the name of the section of town that you lived in.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in a-- in a town, which is-- which was called Prague 18. The Prague town was divided into sections. Like here in New York, we have Brooklyn, Queens, Manhattan.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had-- I was in Prague 18, which was called Stresovice. It was an, an older section where I lived. There was also a newer section. The Jewish section was mainly Prague 1, which was called Josefov.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where the ghetto, the original ghetto, used to be. And that's presently is the, the-- the Jewish cemetery is there also, and the synagogues. Now, our-- our-- my parents belonged to a synagogue, which was a modern synagogue. It was in Prague, in Smichov, which was-- and because my grandfather was Orthodox, it was closer to walk there. It was only about like four miles instead of going, going like seven miles to Prague. So we, we belonged to that synagogue. That synagogue later on was used as a storage house during the Germans, and they put all kinds of things in it. I, I went there after the war and it just is standing there. It's, It's no more a synagogue. It's just-- I don't know what they converted it to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your mother, your father, what kind of people they were, and a little bit about the house. What did it look like? How did you celebrate Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my, father only went through secondary school, which was about nine years of, of schooling, and then he had a trade school. However, my mother was-- had a business school. And so she was-- she was really keeping-- when my father had the business, so she was keeping the books for him and did all the arrangements. He just ran the-- ran the business. We, we-- because when my grandfather was alive, I remember we were celebrating holidays. I used to go to, to, to the temple. And also, I used to attend. Because in Czechoslovakia, there was no separation of the churches. So most of the, the Catholic kids and the Protestant kids, they were able to attend school, their religious education in the school. But I had to go once a week, this I recall, it was always Tuesdays, to, to another school from me, which was about, well, I guess, about seven miles. And there was a cantor, his name was Greenbaum. And he used to teach us Hebrew and, and, also the religious history, Bible stories and stuff like that. And so my-- I-- my father was really involved in, in his business. And we were not associated too much with the Jewish life because it was-- we were remote from that and there were-- most of the Jewish population really lived downtown in the ghetto area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did you celebrate any Jewish holidays when you were seven, eight nine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We, we-- when--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We never had a seder. We had seder only when my grandparents were alive. But after my grandfather died, we didn't celebrate seder. My, my father was not really religious. None of the children-- my grandfather had seven living children. He had total of nine, two died. And none of them became religious. None of them celebrated seder. The reason being, they got mostly assimilated because we-- they lived among Christians. So they were assimilated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do as a child? Do you remember what games you played or what things you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to play soccer during the, during the spring and, and fall. And during the winter, we used to play hockey. Those were the main sports, which I-- which I played when I was a young kid. And we spent a lot of time on, on the soccer field with the kids. I had a lot of Christian I had a lot of friends, whom I am still in contact today, who are still living, who were my friends. Most of them from that era are Christians.

speaker: interviewer
text: When, when, you were a boy in the 1930s, when you are seven, eight, nine, 10--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any feeling of anti-Semitism, or was that just absent from your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in the area where I was, the Czech weren't anti-Semitic in that-- in that area. I can't say I ever felt, being-- you know, being discriminated in any way. What happened is when Hitler came to power and there was an upheaval in Europe, a lot of Polish Jews came to, to Prague. A lot of German Jews came to Prague. And they started to speak German and started to speak also Yiddish. And to the Czechs, Germans were something which was-- which was always against the Czechs. And they associated the Jews with the Germans, and that's when you started to see little anti-Semitic talks, because of the language. And also, some of them were Orthodox. I have to say that lot of-- before this upheaval, a lot of Jews in, in Czechoslovakia didn't-- weren't really true orthodox, wearing the black, black hats and coats. So there was not much. And on top of that, they were mostly in the center of Prague, downtown, so there was not much anti-Semitic. The anti-Semitism really came through Eastern Czechoslovakia where also there was a trial at one time after the, the war. A Jewish fellow was accused of during Passover that he killed somebody and used the blood for--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was before the war or after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no this is after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. This was after. This is-- and at that time, the President of Czechoslovakia, Masaryk, stepped in, and he defended because there is no such thing, that, you know, Jews take blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in the-- in the late 1930s when you were still a small boy--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall hearing about what was going on in Germany? Was there news? Were people concerned or frightened in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- people in Czechoslovakia always thought that something like that cannot happen, the Jews in Czechoslovakia, cannot happen among the Czechs. And there was no-- there was-- we, we knew what was going on in Germany. Also, I had an uncle who was in, in, in a business. It was a jewelry business. And in 1938 when, when, when the Germans came to Prague, he packed up and went to Switzerland. And he was telling his brothers, look, there is-- look what's happening in Germany. You know, it's going to happen here. But nobody, at that time, imagined that something like this could-- would have happened. And so all, all my-- all my uncles and aunts, they stayed with the exception of this one brother who went to Switzerland, and to England, and eventually to the United States. And that's the uncle who brought me over, over here after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what, what was the first moment, you must have been about 11 or 12, when you understood as a little boy, perhaps through your parents, that bad things indeed were happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, what-- the first thing what happened is when we started to wear-- started to wear stars. And what happened is, as a kid, all of a sudden, you imagine all kinds of things. I remember one time when I was going to school in, in Prague, I was in the back and there was a little old lady who had chocolate. I haven't had chocolate in, I don't know, about two years. And she offered me a bar of chocolate, and I, I was so suspicious. I didn't take the chocolate because I thought it was poison. So I, I started to get suspicious of everybody because you got kicked out of the trolley cars. And on top of that, one day the Jews had to get inside the house at eight o'clock. There was a curfew after eight o'clock. And we always used to sit in front of our house and speak to the people around because-- the Christian people-- because they knew my father. My father grew up with them. I grew up with my friends. So they stopped and we were just BS-ing, or talking, talking around. But one day we were sitting after eight o'clock. Oh, during this time, nobody bothered us after eight o'clock. And this one day, my father's friend, who joined the Czech [NON-ENGLISH] it was a Nazi party. A friend. My father had one time loaned money to to go into business. All of a sudden, he came to, to-- in front of-- to my father and he says, get out, you, you lousy Jews. Get inside the house. And my father, at that time, swore that when he comes back, if he ever-- you know, if he ever survives this, this mess, he's going to do something to him, And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was-- what year was it that you had to start wearing the star? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall the year, exactly the year, but it was not long after, after the Germans came. You know, you tried always-- I used to-- I was very conscious of it and I would always try to put books over it. And, you know, sometimes you got stopped with SS men, and, and they would just kick you out from the, from the trolley cars or wherever you were.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say when the Germans came. What did you see when they came? How did they come to Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Well, what happened is I recall, we used to-- this was in, I think, it was March when they came in 1938. March or-- it was beginning of the spring. And all of a sudden, the radio-- we had the radio on. And they said, the, the German troops are marching to Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every-- I recall that my mother was crying. We used to have a maid, who was a Jewish girl from, from the eastern part of Romelia, from Czechoslovakia, and she was crying. And right away, she said she's going to go home and because she can't-- she doesn't know what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and that's-- not long after that, my father-- my uncle lost his business. He was also a contractor. And, and the guy, the so-called forvalter, the administrator-- we used to call him Herr Forvalter-- came to our house. And he said, your, your business and all the equipment will be moved from your house. All the trucks and the horses and combined with my uncle's who lived about three miles away from us and had a--

speaker: interviewee
text: So they combined the businesses together. And my father and my uncle, they were advisors to him because this guy didn't know anything about the business, what, what to do. So they were his advisors. And, and the guy conducted the business, and so my father didn't do anything after that. The Germans came to Prague in March of 1939. You were about 10.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939? Yeah, I think, yes, that's right. I thought it was '38. Yeah, '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened in the couple years after that? How did this lead up to your being transported to Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened is my-- the uncle who had this business, he had three children. His oldest, oldest son, Carl, was, was called for a-- into a transport, with a transport, used to call it AK1, which was an [NON-ENGLISH ] one, which was going to prepare the, the Theresienstadt, which was a-- at this-- this-- Theresienstadt is a town, which is surrounded. It was built during the Maria Theresa time. And it's surrounded by a [INAUDIBLE] wall, so it suited itself for a closed city because the only way you could get out is through some gates. And, and then one side was open. The side to the [NON-ENGLISH], which was the area where the transport used to come in, that was opened up. But otherwise, it was surrounded by moats with double walls. And the moat was in between. There were some little water in between and catacombs went through these. It was a real, real old town, and it had barracks. It was used as a military town and it had something like seven different barracks. I recall some of the names, Dresden, Hamlich Sudeten-Kaserne, and Jaeger-Kaserne, and Magdeburg-Kaserne. These were all barracks, military barracks. And it was in an area where the Czech section was bordering on the German-- on the German population, the Sudetenland. Also, not far away was [NON-ENGLISH], which-- which in German, was [GERMAN], which was a pretty large Jewish, Jewish town. So the Germans built-- wanted to build a, a ghetto. Next to the town was a so-called [NON-ENGLISH], a little, little fortress, which was used as a prison for political prisoners. And that was just, just bordering on a-- on a town. And so the Germans took these 1,000 guys, young guys, all of them are young guys in the beginning '20s, and-- to build up this house. So what they were doing is cleaning out the barracks, putting bunks in. Then later on, the Germans moved out the population, because among the barracks there were also houses. So the population was moved out, and the whole town was converted into a, a ghetto, where only Jews were brought in. Next transport was AK2, which was another group of young guys that were helping out. And then they started to ship and transport from all. I was-- I was-- I came with a transport, AAQ, and my number was AAQ463. And, and we came to this [NON-ENGLISH], which is in German, [GERMAN], and in Czech. We were assembled in Prague. There used to be an exhibition palace, they called it an exhibition palace, in Prague, which they converted into a section where they-- all the Jews would come in. And then from there, then they would ship them into this Auschwitz. I mean, Theresienstadt. And when you were allowed to take, I guess, two bags of-- they put us into cattle wagons, and, and we traveled to-- My grandmother also, who was, at that time 80 years old, she was unable to walk. So they brought in a moving van. And the poor woman, when she got into the assembly area, she was all, all banged up because she-- they put her on a chair. And as the moving van was going around, she fell down and she was going from wall to wall. And she was all banged up. And so my father was trying to, you know, bandage her up. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this transport--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was just you? Or your father, your mother, your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This transport was the whole family. It was my, my father, my mother, my sister and myself, and my grandmother. The whole building where we were just was evacuated. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was the trip from Prague to Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I don't recall. Maybe four hours or something like this because we were stopping all over the place. But maybe four, maybe five hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when you arrived? Do you remember arrival there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we-- when we arrived, what happened there were SS men there and Czech gendarmes were over there. And they-- we went out. They, they took most of the-- there was some Jewish guys also, these guys who were-- young guys-- with little two-wheel wagons. And they loaded up all these baggages on top of these wagons and we marched for, I think, it's about approximately a mile into the town. And they put us into one of these barracks. And what happened, I remember the first thing what happened because my, my cousin knew already-- he was there from the beginning. He knew how to-- --go around. So he said to my father, look, get yourself some kind of an old bag and fill it up with straw so you can sleep. At least you don't have to sleep on the floor. So you can sleep on, on a straw with these.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father, he brought him some, some [INAUDIBLE]. And my father filled it up, so we were able to sleep. And then he had to select-- when he was in Theresienstadt, he had to select what he's going to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since my father was a, a contractor, during Sundays he used to, instead of hiring blacksmiths-- Excuse me. He would hoof his own horses. So he was familiar how to hoof horses. So this cousin of, of mine, he suggested that maybe he should become a blacksmith because there is no Jewish blacksmith. There is only one blacksmith in the town, and they have quite a few horses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, so he became a blacksmith and that, I think, saved our lives, the whole family's life. OK, we'll continue your experience in Theresienstadt on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
